title: Gowning, Hand Hygiene, and Aseptic Technique
description: For community pharmacy technicians, maintaining sterility is essential when compounding sterile preparations. This requires following standard operating procedures (SOPs) based on guidelines like USP 797. The process strictly follows a "dirtiest to cleanest" sequence: Removing personal items, garbing from head to toe, vigorous handwashing, gowning, and finally donning sterile gloves.1. Pre-Gowning PreparationBefore entering the ante-room or buffer room, you must eliminate potential sources of contamination:Remove Personal Items: Take off all hand and wrist jewelry, watches, visible piercings, and makeup. Outer garments (sweaters, coats) must be left outside the sterile compounding area.Nails and Hair: Ensure fingernails are trimmed short (usually \(1/4\) inch or less) and free of polish. Tie hair back and contain it completely within a head/hair cover.2. The Garbing Sequence (Dirtiest to Cleanest)Garbing order is highly specific to your facility's SOPs. Generally, follow these steps:Shoe Covers: Put on dedicated shoe covers to avoid bringing outside floor contamination into the sterile area.Hair/Facial Covers: Don your bouffant cap and beard cover (if applicable).Face Mask: Put on a surgical-style face mask or N95, ensuring both your nose and mouth are fully covered.Eye Protection: Put on goggles or face shields (required for hazardous or irritant compounding).3. Hand Hygiene & ScrubbingHand hygiene is the most important step for microbe reduction. Perform hand hygiene exactly according to your facility's protocol:Clean under nails: Use a disposable nail cleaner under running warm water to remove debris from beneath your fingernails.Vigorous wash: Wash hands and forearms up to the elbows for at least 30 seconds using facility-approved antiseptic soap.Dry completely: Dry hands and forearms entirely with lint-free wipes or a low-lint disposable towel. Do not use standard hand dryers or reusable scrub brushes.4. GowningAfter your hands and forearms are completely dry, don your gown:Material: Put on a non-shedding, disposable gown with sleeves that fit snugly around the wrists and an enclosed neck.Reusability: Non-hazardous compounding gowns may be reused if unsoiled and stored properly, but they can never leave the designated clean area.5. GlovingSterile gloves are your last barrier of defense before handling compounded sterile products:Donning: Put on sterile gloves in a manner that maintains sterility, ensuring they are pulled completely over the cuffs of your gown.Disinfection: Sanitize gloves frequently with sterile 70% isopropyl alcohol and let them air dry before starting any sterile manipulations. Replace gloves immediately if torn, punctured, or contaminated.Aseptic Technique Best PracticesWhen manipulating sterile products within the ISO Class 5 Primary Engineering Control (PEC), follow these operational rules:First Air: Keep all critical sites directly in the path of the unidirectional HEPA-filtered "first air." Never block the airflow with your hands or supplies.Distance: Work at least 6 inches inside the lip of the laminar flow hood to prevent backwash contamination from room air.Disinfection: Wipe down all surfaces, vial septa, and equipment ports with sterile 70% isopropyl alcohol prior to use.
